title: Comparative Sensory Processing
description: A graduate course on how sensory systems solve their computational problems — and on what the comparison between modalities reveals about olfaction.
intro: Most sensory neuroscience is taught one modality at a time, as a tour of anatomy: receptors, then relays, then cortex. This course is organised the other way round. Each module begins with a computational problem — what must the animal infer, from what, under what loss, on what deadline — and treats the biology as evidence about how that problem is solved.

The reason for doing it this way is comparative. Vision and audition have mature normative theories: we can derive retinal receptive fields and cochlear filters from the statistics of natural input and check the answer against the biology. Olfaction has no theory of comparable maturity. The wager of this course is that this gap is informative, and that the clearest route to a theory of smell runs through the modalities that already have one.

So every module uses the same eleven-section skeleton, in the same order, so that §4 always means the receptor array whether you are reading about cones or odorant receptors. You should be able to read across modules as easily as down one. Where a structure is absent — and olfaction is missing several that vision teaches you to expect — the section says so and asks why.
homeNote: Eight modalities in full, all using the identical eleven-section skeleton so they can be read across as easily as down, plus the three synthesis modules: common motifs, key differences, and the capstone. The capstone ends with a ranked research agenda — the course's deliverable.

modules.label: Module 0
modules.title: The Normative Toolkit
modules.blurb: Why the comparison works, the lessons it yields, and the shared vocabulary the modality modules assume.

subsections.title: Why compare modalities
subsections.blurb: Sensory systems as natural experiments, and the seven lessons the course is built around.

subsections.title: Three normative stances
subsections.blurb: Efficient coding, Bayesian inference, and task optimality make different predictions. Telling them apart is the core skill.

subsections.title: Efficient coding in two regimes
subsections.blurb: Whiten at high SNR, pool at low SNR — the cleanest quantitative prediction in sensory neuroscience.

subsections.title: Module quiz — The Normative Toolkit
subsections.blurb: Six questions on the three stances, the five criteria, and the SNR reversal.

modules.blurb: The reference modality. Taught first and deepest, because it supplies the templates everything else is measured against.

subsections.title: Routing — the thalamus and what it is for

subsections.title: The central representation — V1
subsections.blurb: Sparse coding derives V1-like receptive fields — the deepest any normative theory has reached, which is not very deep.

subsections.title: Module quiz — Vision
subsections.blurb: Eight questions on what vision established and what it hands forward.

modules.label: Module 8
modules.title: Exotic Senses
modules.blurb: Electroreception, echolocation, infrared, magnetoreception — natural experiments that populate the design space the familiar five leave empty.

subsections.title: Exotic senses as natural experiments
subsections.blurb: Each isolates one design variable the familiar five confound.

subsections.title: Electroreception — cancelling the predictable
subsections.blurb: The clearest circuit-level implementation of subtracting a prediction.

subsections.title: Echolocation — designing the experiment
subsections.blurb: Real-time probe control, and maps of computed variables.

subsections.title: Infrared sensing — acuity as deconvolution
subsections.blurb: Terrible optics, good performance, recovered computationally.

subsections.title: Magnetoreception — a sense with no known receptor
subsections.blurb: What the difficulty teaches about grounding a sensory channel.

subsections.title: Synthesis — what the exotic senses hand olfaction
subsections.blurb: Three mechanistic templates, each with a runnable experiment.

subsections.title: Module quiz — Exotic Senses
subsections.blurb: Four natural experiments, and what each holds constant that the familiar five confound.

modules.label: Module 9
modules.title: Development and Evolution
modules.blurb: The two constraint sources the course assumed and never examined — how a system must be built, and how it got here. Convergence between insects and vertebrates is the strongest normative argument olfaction has; developmental specifiability is where the missing metric actually costs the animal something.

subsections.title: Why development and evolution belong in a normative course
subsections.blurb: Three kinds of explanation — optimality, developmental accessibility, historical contingency — and how to tell them apart.

subsections.title: What a genome can specify
subsections.blurb: A genome writes rules, not connections. A rule needs an ordering — which is where the missing metric imposes a cost rather than an absence.

subsections.title: The receptor repertoire as an evolving object
subsections.blurb: Birth-and-death evolution, pseudogenes, and the genomic alternative that makes the capstone's abundance null interpretable.

subsections.title: Convergent solutions
subsections.blurb: Insects and vertebrates built the same architecture from non-homologous parts. What that licenses, what it does not, and the three objections weighed.

subsections.title: Critical periods, plasticity, and adult neurogenesis
subsections.blurb: Olfaction is the outlier at a depth no other sensory system matches — and the non-stationarity explanation is currently a story, not a result.

subsections.title: Synthesis and the olfaction hook
subsections.blurb: Scoring the three arguments, the developmental datasheet row, and six clues handed to the capstone.

subsections.title: Module quiz — Development and Evolution
subsections.blurb: Eight questions on constraint, convergence, and the discipline of not telling stories.

modules.label: Module 10
modules.title: Common Motifs
modules.blurb: The seven lessons promised in §0.0, discharged against the assembled comparative record. Five motif families, each ending in a concrete falsifiable prediction for olfaction, with the absent-versus-unmeasured distinction as the running theme.

subsections.title: Sampling
subsections.blurb: Active sensing, the 4–12 Hz band and its three competing explanations, efference copy, and sampling as optimal experiment design. Lessons 1 and 5.

subsections.title: Early computation
subsections.blurb: Divisive normalisation, whitening and its SNR reversal, convergence for signal-to-noise, and predictive subtraction — four operations with four different derivations. Lessons 1, 2 and 5.

subsections.title: The central representation
subsections.blurb: Expansion recoding, sparseness, recurrence, and maps — four motifs the vision-trained reader receives as one package, separated by the modality that has three of them and not the fourth. Lesson 3.

subsections.title: Dynamics
subsections.blurb: Adaptation across four decades of time, splitting into complementary channels for three distinct reasons, and how a population compensates for a sensor slower than its signal. Lessons 4 and 7.

subsections.title: Architecture
subsections.blurb: Pathway depth against deadlines, thalamus decomposed into services, stream splits and their anatomical signatures, and the consumer that determines the code. Lessons 6 and 7, and the ledger for all seven.

subsections.title: Module quiz — Common Motifs
subsections.blurb: Genuinely absent, or never looked for? Eight questions on the distinction that runs the module.

modules.label: Module 11
modules.title: Key Differences
modules.blurb: Five quantitative axes from the comparative datasheet, each stated as a prediction and checked against all eight modalities — including where it fails.

subsections.title: Ordering and metric
subsections.blurb: The course's central claim, audited motif by motif and then attacked — six of fourteen motifs never needed a metric at all.

subsections.title: Dimensionality
subsections.blurb: From D = 1 to D ≈ 1000. Why high dimensionality is olfaction's handicap for identity and its advantage for demixing — the same number, opposite signs.

subsections.title: Timescale and the sensor–signal ratio
subsections.blurb: A sensor an order of magnitude too slow for structure animals demonstrably use, and the one other sense with the same problem — which has already solved it.

subsections.title: Depth and routing
subsections.blurb: Two synapses to cortex, five to action, no obligatory relay. Testing the antiquity explanation against spinocerebellar bypass, and reporting what survives.

subsections.title: What the consumer wants
subsections.blurb: The loss function as a design variable — the only axis here that never fails, and why that should make you uneasy rather than satisfied.

subsections.title: Module quiz — Key Differences
subsections.blurb: Holding a weakened claim precisely, including the axis whose perfect record is a symptom.

modules.label: Module 12
modules.title: Artificial Sensing
modules.blurb: The course's central claim, tested outside biology. Machine vision and audition succeeded after their corpora were built; machine olfaction stalled at the same place the theory did — in systems with no evolution, no metabolism and no anatomy to blame.

subsections.title: Why artificial systems are evidence about biology
subsections.blurb: What a working artefact licenses you to conclude about an organism, and what a stalled one does — five rungs of inference, of which this module stands on three.

subsections.title: What vision and audition got from their datasets
subsections.blurb: The corpus came first, assembled by people who did not know what would exploit it — plus the convergence of trained networks on cortex-like early filters.

subsections.title: Machine olfaction, and why it stalled
subsections.blurb: Electronic noses adopted the biological array design on purpose. Drift and cross-sensitivity are individually survivable and jointly fatal, and the stall is at the input representation.

subsections.title: The same asymmetry, outside biology
subsections.blurb: The independent test, four rival explanations stated at full strength, and the honest verdict — supported and not isolated, with the five measurements that would change that.

subsections.title: What a solved machine olfaction would need
subsections.blurb: Gaps 1 and 4 as an engineering specification — and the finding that the cheapest subset is the one buying the normative theory rather than the working device.

subsections.title: Normative synthesis and the olfaction hook
subsections.blurb: Scoring the module's own argument against the five criteria it has applied to everyone else, which it fails on the first — and seven clues to the capstone, one contradicting the thesis.

subsections.title: Module quiz — Artificial Sensing
subsections.blurb: Eight questions on an argument deliberately weaker than it first appears, several of which ask you to attack it.

modules.label: Module 13
modules.title: Olfaction in the Mirror
modules.blurb: For each imported concept: the analogue, the disanalogy that breaks it, and a concrete falsifiable prediction. Ends with a ranked, costed research agenda.

subsections.title: Imports from vision and audition
subsections.blurb: The method, and the two modalities with mature normative theories.

subsections.title: Imports from touch and taste
subsections.blurb: A map does not imply a spatial code; and unordered does not entail high-dimensional.

subsections.title: Imports from the vestibular and proprioceptive systems
subsections.blurb: The closest structural match to olfaction's central ambiguity, and a different job description for a first circuit.

subsections.title: Imports from the exotic senses
subsections.blurb: Cancellation with the mechanism known, hyperacuity from slow elements, and localisation as blind deconvolution.

subsections.title: The research agenda
subsections.blurb: Everything the comparison generated, ranked by informativeness per unit cost — with the null results costed too.

subsections.title: Conclusion — gaps, open questions, and where to read next
subsections.blurb: The four structural gaps behind every experiment in §13.5, the questions the course could not answer, and an annotated route into the literature.

subsections.title: Final exam
subsections.blurb: Eleven cumulative questions, none answerable from a single module — and three free-response questions that reward saying what is uncertain.
